summary:
Tell es-Sultan is the archaeological mound at ancient Jericho, in the Jordan Valley near the Ain es-Sultan spring, that has been excavated by successive generations of archaeologists beginning with Charles Warren in 1867–1868 and most comprehensively by Kathleen Kenyon in the 1950s. The mound's earliest levels — predating the substantial PPNA settlement — reveal Natufian and proto-Neolithic occupation from approximately 10,000 BCE, before the construction of the major architecture that would make [[event:2|Jericho]] famous.

The proto-Jericho phase at Tell es-Sultan shows evidence of a small, seasonal or semi-permanent camp of [[event:4|Natufian]] hunters and gatherers who returned repeatedly to exploit the resources around the perennial spring. This pre-agricultural occupation laid the demographic and locational foundation for the later PPNA village that grew into the most impressive early Neolithic settlement known in the southern Levant. The stratigraphic sequence at Tell es-Sultan from Natufian base through PPNA and PPNB layers provides one of the clearest and most detailed archaeological records of the transition from mobile foraging to settled agriculture available anywhere in the world.
